text: The nuclear radius is related to mass number \(A\) by:
explanation: Nuclear radius: \( R = R_0 A^{1/3} \approx 1.2 A^{1/3}\,\text{fm} \). This implies nuclear density is constant (independent of A).
name: Nuclear Radius

text: The binding energy of a nucleus is related to mass defect \(\Delta m\) by:
explanation: Binding energy: \( BE = \Delta m \cdot c^2 \), where \( \Delta m = Zm_p + Nm_n - m_{nucleus} \) (mass defect). 1 amu = 931.5 MeV/c².
name: Binding Energy Formula

text: The Q-value of a nuclear reaction is:
options: Energy released = (mass of reactants - mass of products) × c²
explanation: Q-value: \( Q = (m_i - m_f)c^2 \). Exothermic: Q > 0, endothermic: Q < 0. Energy released/absorbed in nuclear reaction.
name: Q-Value of Nuclear Reaction

text: The law of radioactive decay is:
explanation: Radioactive decay law: \( N(t) = N_0 e^{-\lambda t} \), where \(\lambda\) = decay constant. First-order process like chemical kinetics.
name: Radioactive Decay Law

text: The half-life \(T_{1/2}\) of a radioactive substance is related to decay constant \(\lambda\) by:
explanation: Half-life: \( T_{1/2} = \ln 2/\lambda = 0.693/\lambda \). After \(n\) half-lives: \( N = N_0/2^n \).
name: Half-Life Formula

text: In alpha decay, the nucleus loses:
options: A helium nucleus (2 protons + 2 neutrons): \( ^A_Z X \to ^{A-4}_{Z-2}Y + ^4_2He \)
explanation: Alpha decay: \( ^A_Z X \to ^{A-4}_{Z-2}Y + ^4_2\alpha \). A decreases by 4, Z decreases by 2. Example: \( ^{238}_{92}U \to ^{234}_{90}Th + ^4_2He \).
name: Alpha Decay

options: A proton converts to neutron + positron + neutrino
A neutron converts to proton + electron (\(\beta^-\)) + antineutrino: \( n \to p + e^- + \bar{\nu} \)
explanation: \(\beta^-\) decay: \( n \to p + e^- + \bar{\nu}_e \). A unchanged, Z increases by 1. Example: \( ^{14}_6C \to ^{14}_7N + e^- + \bar{\nu} \).
name: Beta-Minus Decay

options: An excited nucleus transitions to lower energy state (no change in A or Z)
explanation: Gamma emission: excited nucleus (after \(\alpha\) or \(\beta\) decay or neutron capture) releases excess energy as \(\gamma\) photon. No change in A or Z.
name: Gamma Decay

text: The activity \(A\) of a radioactive sample is:
explanation: Activity: \( A = \lambda N = \lambda N_0 e^{-\lambda t} = A_0 e^{-\lambda t} \). Unit: Becquerel (Bq) = 1 decay/s. 1 Curie = 3.7×10¹⁰ Bq.
name: Radioactive Activity

text: Nuclear fission is the process where:
options: Two light nuclei combine to form heavier nucleus
A heavy nucleus splits into two medium-mass nuclei with release of energy
Neutron is captured without energy release
explanation: Nuclear fission: heavy nucleus (U-235, Pu-239) absorbs neutron → splits into two fragments + 2-3 neutrons + ~200 MeV energy. Basis of nuclear reactors.
name: Nuclear Fission

options: Very high temperature (\( \sim 10^7\) K) to overcome Coulomb barrier
explanation: Fusion: two light nuclei (e.g., H isotopes) fuse to form heavier nucleus with large energy release. Requires ~10⁷ K for thermonuclear fusion. Powers the Sun.
name: Nuclear Fusion Condition

text: The mean life \(\tau\) of a radioactive substance is related to half-life by:
explanation: Mean life: \( \tau = 1/\lambda = T_{1/2}/\ln 2 = 1.44\,T_{1/2} \). After time \(\tau\): activity reduces to \(1/e\) of initial.
name: Mean Life and Half-Life

text: The critical mass in a nuclear reactor is the minimum mass of fissile material required for:
explanation: Critical mass: minimum mass for self-sustaining chain reaction (multiplication factor \(k=1\)). Sub-critical: \(k<1\). Super-critical: \(k>1\).
name: Critical Mass

options: Fission of U-235 by slow (thermal) neutron
explanation: This is the fission of U-235 by thermal neutron producing Ba-141, Kr-92, 3 neutrons, and ~200 MeV energy. These 3 neutrons can trigger more fissions (chain reaction).
name: U-235 Fission Reaction

text: Radiocarbon dating uses the isotope \( ^{14}C \) with half-life \( \approx 5730\) years. It can date:
options: Living organisms and organic remains up to ~50,000 years
explanation: \( ^{14}C \) dating: living organisms maintain constant \( ^{14}C/^{12}C \) ratio (by photosynthesis). After death, \( ^{14}C \) decays. From remaining ratio, age is determined.
name: Radiocarbon Dating
